title: Vector Databases, Atlas Vector Search, and Voyage Embeddings
objective: Frame the deck around benchmarkable retrieval behavior.
coreClaim: Vector database choice should be evaluated through a fixed workload, fixed embeddings, fixed labels, and explicit metrics.
practicalTakeaway: Do not compare vendor claims; compare measured retrieval behavior.

title: Learning Goals
objective: Set the technical goals for the deck.
coreClaim: A useful evaluation separates embeddings, indexes, filtering, reranking, operations, and cost.
practicalTakeaway: Keep the benchmark surfaces separate.

title: Running Workload
objective: Define support/customer-ticket retrieval as the anchor.
coreClaim: Support-ticket search stresses semantic recall, exact terms, metadata filters, freshness, and tenant isolation.
practicalTakeaway: Benchmark filtered and unfiltered retrieval.

title: What A Vector Database Actually Owns
objective: Separate storage/index responsibilities from embeddings and application logic.
coreClaim: A vector database owns vector storage, ANN/exact search, metadata filtering, update/delete behavior, and serving operations, not embedding quality.
practicalTakeaway: Do not credit the database for embedding model behavior.

title: Benchmark Harness Topology
coreClaim: The harness fixes corpus, labels, Voyage embeddings, queries, filters, and metric code while swapping store adapters.
practicalTakeaway: The adapter is the only thing that should change across vector stores.

title: Corpus Shape
coreClaim: Support tickets should be benchmarked as documents with text, comments, resolution, product area, priority, tenant, and timestamps.
practicalTakeaway: Preserve metadata needed by real filters.

title: Query Set
objective: Define what queries should look like.
coreClaim: Queries should include natural-language symptoms plus exact identifiers and structured constraints.
practicalTakeaway: Support search is rarely pure semantic nearest neighbor search.

title: Relevance Judgments
coreClaim: A benchmark without qrels measures latency but not retrieval quality.
practicalTakeaway: Create ticket-level or graded relevance labels before tuning stores.

title: Voyage Embeddings
coreClaim: Voyage embeddings should be treated as a fixed input artifact for vector-store comparison.
practicalTakeaway: Cache vectors so database runs do not include embedding variability.

title: Retrieval Pipeline
coreClaim: A benchmark run embeds a query, applies filters, retrieves candidates, optionally reranks, and calculates metrics.
practicalTakeaway: Measure first-stage and reranked quality separately.

title: ANN Versus Exact Search
coreClaim: ANN improves latency by trading away exhaustive comparison; exact search is useful for ground truth and small filtered sets.
practicalTakeaway: Use exact search or high-recall settings to calibrate ANN loss.

title: HNSW Mental Model
coreClaim: HNSW-style indexes search a navigable neighbor graph instead of scanning every vector.
practicalTakeaway: Tune recall/latency through index and search parameters where exposed.

title: IVF, Disk, And Quantization
objective: Explain alternative index and compression families.
coreClaim: IVF, DiskANN-style indexes, and quantization exist to manage memory, storage, and latency at larger scales.
practicalTakeaway: Benchmark scale and memory footprint, not just top-k latency.

title: Distance Metrics
coreClaim: Embedding model normalization and index distance metric must align across stores.
practicalTakeaway: Standardize cosine, dot product, or euclidean semantics before comparing.

title: Chunking Tickets
coreClaim: Ticket-level, comment-level, and resolution-level chunks produce different retrieval behavior.
practicalTakeaway: Benchmark chunking as a retrieval design variable.

title: Metric Map
coreClaim: Quality, latency, throughput, cost, freshness, and correctness must not collapse into one score.
practicalTakeaway: Report a scorecard, not a winner headline.

title: Recall@k
coreClaim: Recall@k tells whether the relevant ticket appears in the candidate set.
practicalTakeaway: Use Recall@50 before reranking because the reranker cannot recover missing candidates.

title: nDCG And MRR
coreClaim: nDCG rewards graded ranking quality; MRR rewards placing the first relevant result early.
practicalTakeaway: Use nDCG@10 and MRR@10 for analyst-facing search quality.

title: Latency And Throughput
coreClaim: p50, p95, p99 latency and QPS under concurrency should be reported separately from relevance.
practicalTakeaway: Do warm and cold runs; report concurrency.

title: Cost Model
coreClaim: Cost includes embeddings, reranking, database compute/storage, operational overhead, and index rebuild/update work.
practicalTakeaway: A cheap vector query can still be expensive after embedding and reranking.

title: Python Harness Interfaces
coreClaim: All stores should implement the same Python adapter methods for create, upsert, query, delete, and stats.
practicalTakeaway: The harness should make store-specific behavior explicit at the boundary.

title: Adapter Contract
coreClaim: Adapter parity means identical ids, vectors, metadata, filters, requested top-k, and result normalization.
practicalTakeaway: Normalize result shape before metric calculation.

title: MongoDB Atlas Vector Search
coreClaim: MongoDB Atlas Vector Search co-locates vectors with operational documents and supports retrieval that composes with MongoDB/Atlas Search patterns.
practicalTakeaway: Benchmark MongoDB as an operational data plus vector search platform.

title: MongoDB Benchmark Path
coreClaim: A MongoDB adapter should store ticket fields, metadata, and vectors in one document and query through the vector index.
practicalTakeaway: Keep support-ticket metadata queryable next to the embedding.

title: Pinecone
coreClaim: Pinecone is a managed vector-first service benchmark with explicit index configuration and metadata filtering.
practicalTakeaway: Measure vector-service ergonomics and filtered retrieval behavior.

title: Weaviate
coreClaim: Weaviate exposes vector indexing, hybrid search, and multi-index concepts that make it useful for semantic plus lexical support search.
practicalTakeaway: Evaluate hybrid behavior separately from pure vector retrieval.

title: Qdrant
coreClaim: Qdrant combines vector search with payload filtering and indexing, making filter selectivity an important evaluation dimension.
practicalTakeaway: Benchmark payload indexes for tenant/product filters.

title: Store Comparison Matrix
objective: Compare systems at a high level.
coreClaim: MongoDB, vector-first systems, Chroma, and pgvector optimize for different ownership and operational boundaries.
practicalTakeaway: Use the matrix to decide what to test, not to choose without testing.

title: Milvus
coreClaim: Milvus is a vector database benchmark target for index-family breadth, scale-oriented deployment, and filtered search behavior.
practicalTakeaway: Include Milvus when scale and index family choice are central.

title: Chroma
coreClaim: Chroma is valuable for local development and baseline harness workflows, but should be labeled differently from managed production services.
practicalTakeaway: Use Chroma to develop the benchmark before scaling the test matrix.

title: pgvector
coreClaim: pgvector is strongest when vector search must live inside Postgres with SQL filters, transactions, and existing relational data.
practicalTakeaway: Benchmark SQL filter plans and index choices, not only vector distance.

title: Hybrid Search
coreClaim: Support search often needs hybrid search because exact error codes, account ids, product names, and invoice terms matter.
practicalTakeaway: Add a hybrid track to the benchmark rather than judging pure vector search alone.

title: Filter Selectivity
objective: Explain why filters change benchmark behavior.
coreClaim: Tenant, product, priority, and date filters can change both recall and latency because they restrict candidate generation and ranking.
practicalTakeaway: Run low-, medium-, and high-selectivity filter suites.

title: Freshness And Updates
coreClaim: Support tickets evolve, so ingest-only benchmarks miss update, delete, and visibility behavior.
practicalTakeaway: Measure update lag and deletion visibility.

title: Reranking With Voyage
coreClaim: Voyage reranking should be measured as a second-stage quality improvement over each store's candidate set.
practicalTakeaway: Compare top-50 candidate recall before reranking and top-10 quality after reranking.

title: Failure Analysis
coreClaim: Benchmark tables identify differences; failure analysis explains why they happen.
practicalTakeaway: Sample false negatives, false positives, filter misses, and stale results.

title: Experiment Matrix
coreClaim: A practical comparison varies store, top-k, filters, hybrid mode, reranking, corpus scale, and concurrency deliberately.
practicalTakeaway: Keep one experiment variable changing at a time.

title: Reporting
coreClaim: The report should separate candidate recall, final ranking, latency, ingest, updates, cost, and operational notes.
practicalTakeaway: A scorecard is harder to misread than a leaderboard.

title: MongoDB Versus Vector-First
coreClaim: MongoDB's core advantage is operational document context; vector-first systems' core advantage is focused vector-serving ergonomics and tuning.
practicalTakeaway: Choose based on where data ownership and serving complexity should live.

title: MongoDB Plus Voyage
coreClaim: MongoDB Atlas Vector Search plus Voyage embeddings is a strong baseline when support data already lives in MongoDB and retrieval needs both semantic and operational filters.
practicalTakeaway: Start here if MongoDB is already your ticket/customer system of record.

title: When Dedicated Vector DB Wins
coreClaim: Dedicated vector databases are compelling when vector scale, ANN tuning, vector-native operations, or managed vector service ownership dominate.
practicalTakeaway: Move when the benchmark shows store-specific vector behavior matters.

title: When pgvector Or Chroma Wins
coreClaim: pgvector and Chroma can be the right choices when operational simplicity, local development, or SQL-native integration matters more than managed vector specialization.
practicalTakeaway: Do not overbuy infrastructure before workload evidence justifies it.

title: Decision Framework
coreClaim: The right system follows data ownership, filter selectivity, scale, update rate, hybrid needs, and operational team boundaries.
practicalTakeaway: Use the benchmark to choose for your workload, not the average workload.

title: Benchmark Checklist
coreClaim: A trustworthy benchmark is reproducible, labeled, isolated, metric-complete, and inspected qualitatively.
practicalTakeaway: Version every corpus, embedding, query, qrel, adapter, and result artifact.

title: References
coreClaim: The deck is grounded in official vendor docs and retrieval evaluation references.
practicalTakeaway: Use the references to tune the benchmark for exact service versions.
